does: This paper proposes the Hybrid Reward-Cyclic (HRC) model, which decomposes human preferences into orthogonal transitive (scalar reward) and cyclic (vector) components using game-theoretic decomposition. The authors demonstrate that standard RLHF's reliance on transitive scalar rewards fails to capture cyclic preference patterns, while prior work like GPM conflates hierarchy with cyclicity without guaranteeing optimal solutions. Alongside HRC, they introduce Dynamic Self-Play Preference Optimization (DSPPO), an alignment algorithm that frames the problem as a time-varying game progressing toward Nash equilibrium. Experiments on synthetic data show HRC converges faster and achieves higher accuracy than GPM in mixed settings. On RewardBench 2, HRC consistently outperforms Bradley-Terry and GPM baselines, including +1.23% improvement on Gemma-2B-it.
connects: This work builds on recent research recognizing that human preferences exhibit cyclic rather than purely transitive structure, extending prior efforts like the General Preference Model that attempted to capture non-transitive preferences. The paper addresses a theoretical gap in implicit preference formulations that fail to disentangle hierarchical from cyclic components. It connects to broader RLHF alignment literature that has relied on scalar reward models since the foundational work on preference learning from human feedback. The game-theoretic decomposition approach draws on classical preference theory and extends modern preference-based reinforcement learning by making the structure of preferences explicit rather than implicit. The Nash equilibrium framing links to multi-agent game theory applications in AI alignment.
enables: This explicit decomposition of preferences into transitive and cyclic components opens new directions for understanding and modeling human preference structure in LLM alignment. The framework could enable more accurate preference elicitation and representation when human values contain genuinely cyclic elements. Dynamic Self-Play Preference Optimization suggests a pathway toward time-varying or adaptive alignment procedures that evolve policy toward equilibrium rather than fixed targets. The work points toward richer preference models that go beyond scalar rewards, potentially enabling alignment approaches that handle preference cycles, tradeoffs, and context-dependent values more faithfully. Future work might extend this to multi-agent scenarios, incorporate uncertainty in preference decomposition, or develop methods that explicitly optimize for robustness to cyclic preference patterns in LLM behavior.
